name: Lleu Extended
description: Lleu Llaw Gyffes, meaning "Bright One with the Skillful Hand," is a prominent hero in Welsh mythology, primarily featured in the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi. Born under extraordinary circumstances, his birth was orchestrated through the magic of Gwydion to fulfill a prophecy that his mother, Arianrhod, would never have a child of mortal man. Arianrhod, angered by this deception, placed three curses upon him: that he would never have a name unless she gave it to him, that he would never bear arms unless she armed him, and that he would never have a wife of any race that exists on Earth. Gwydion cleverly circumvented these curses through trickery and disguise, securing Lleu's name and arms from his reluctant mother. To overcome the final curse, Gwydion and Math created a beautiful woman named Blodeuwedd from flowers: broom, meadowsweet, and oak. However, Blodeuwedd proved unfaithful and plotted Lleu's death with her lover, Gronw Pebyr. Lleu could only be killed under extremely specific and elaborate circumstances, which Blodeuwedd learned through deception. Gronw Pebyr ambushed Lleu, who was transformed into an eagle as he was struck by the spear. Gwydion eventually found him, nursed him back to health, and restored him to his human form. Lleu then avenged himself by killing Gronw Pebyr and reclaiming his rightful place. His story embodies themes of destiny, cunning, betrayal, and resilience, making him a central figure in Welsh folklore and a symbol of enduring strength and resourcefulness.
subtitle: The Bright One with the Skillful Hand

keyMyths.title: Birth and Curses
keyMyths.description: Lleu's birth was magically orchestrated, leading to Arianrhod cursing him never to have a name, arms, or wife unless she provided them. Gwydion cleverly tricked Arianrhod into bestowing these upon Lleu.

keyMyths.title: Creation of Blodeuwedd
keyMyths.description: To break Arianrhod's curse of no wife, Gwydion and Math created Blodeuwedd from flowers. This act highlights their magical prowess and the lengths they would go to for Lleu's sake.

keyMyths.title: Betrayal and Transformation
keyMyths.description: Blodeuwedd and Gronw Pebyr plotted Lleu's death, ambushing him under specific conditions. Lleu was struck and transformed into an eagle, symbolizing his vulnerability and temporary defeat.

sources.author: Gwyn Jones and Thomas Jones
sources.description: A comprehensive translation and collection of Welsh medieval tales, including the Fourth Branch where Lleu's story is central.
sources.text: A comprehensive translation and collection of Welsh medieval tales, including the Fourth Branch where Lleu's story is central.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Myths and Legends of the Celts
sources.description: Provides context and analysis of Celtic mythology, including the significance of Lleu within the Welsh pantheon.
sources.text: Provides context and analysis of Celtic mythology, including the significance of Lleu within the Welsh pantheon.
